chunks.text: Sorting Algorithms and Their Efficiency Chapter 11 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Basic Sorting Algorithms • Sorting: – Organize a collection of data into either ascending or descending order • Internal sort – Collection of data fits in memory • External sort – Collection of data does not all fit in memory – Must reside on secondary storage © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Basic Sorting Algorithms • The Selection Sort • The Bubble Sort • The Insertion Sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Selection Sort • Select the largest item – Place in last position • Select the next largest item – Place in 2nd to last position • Repeat for remaining unsorted items – Requires several passes over the data © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Selection Sort FIGURE 11-1 A selection sort of an array of five integers © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • To sort an array into ascending order, you first search for the largest item. • Place largest item in the last position of the array • Swap the last item with the largest item, even if these items happen to be identical. • Repeat The Selection Sort LISTING 11-1 An implementation of the selection sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Note this is a nested loop for n The Selection Sort • Analysis – Selection sort is O(n2) – Appropriate for small n – O(n2) grows rapidly • Could be a good choice when – Data moves are costly, – But comparisons are not © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Easy to understand – Minimal additional space required • Cons – O(n2) – slow – Does not preserve relative order of items with equal keys (not stable) – Potentially suitable for low volume only The Bubble Sort • Compares adjacent items – Exchanges them if out of order – Requires several passes over the data • When ordering successive pairs – Largest item bubbles to end of the array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Bubble Sort FIGURE 11-2 First two passes of a bubble sort of an array of five integers © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Bubble Sort LISTING 11-2 An implementation of the bubble sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Note this is a nested loop for n The Bubble Sort • Analysis – Worst case O(n2) – Best case (array already in order) is O(n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Easy to understand – No additional space required – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – O(n2) – slow – Potentially suitable for low volume only The Insertion Sort • Take each item from unsorted region – Insert it into correct order in sorted region © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 11-3 An insertion sort partitions the array into two regions The Insertion Sort FIGURE 11-4 An insertion sort of an array of five integers © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Sorted region The Insertion Sort LISTING 11-3

– O(n2) – slow – Potentially suitable for low volume only The Insertion Sort • Take each item from unsorted region – Insert it into correct order in sorted region © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved FIGURE 11-3 An insertion sort partitions the array into two regions The Insertion Sort FIGURE 11-4 An insertion sort of an array of five integers © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Sorted region The Insertion Sort LISTING 11-3 An implementation of the insertion sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Insertion Sort LISTING 11-3 An implementation of the insertion sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Note this is a nested loop for n The Insertion Sort • Analysis – Worst case O(n2) – Best case (array already in order) is O(n) • Unsuitable for large arrays – Unless already sorted © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Easy to understand – No additional space required – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – O(n2) – slow – Potentially suitable for low volume only Faster Sorting Algorithms • The Merge Sort • The Quick Sort • The Radix Sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Merge Sort FIGURE 11-5 A merge sort with an auxiliary temporary array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Divide and conquer sorting algorithm • Halve the array, sort its halves, and then merge the halves Merge Sort Pseudocode for the merge sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Merge Sort • Analysis – O(n log n) – Worse case O(n log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Performs well even on large number of items – Simple to implement – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – Requires additional memory O(n) – Requires copying of entries The Quick Sort • Another divide-and-conquer algorithm ⁻ Choose a pivot ⁻ Partition an array into items that are oLess than or equal to the pivot and oThose that are greater than or equal to the pivot ⁻ Recursively apply the same process to the partitioned sub-arrays ⁻ Recursion stops when there is one element remaining in the sub-array © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-9 A partition about a pivot © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved Quick sort algorithm 1. Choose a pivot 2. Partition the array 3. Recursively call The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 1. Choose a pivot 2. Start left to right, find 1st entry that is >= pivot 3. Start right to left, find 1st entry that is <= pivot 4. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 5. Continue left and right searches The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 6. Find next entry that is >= pivot 7. Find next entry that is <= pivot 8. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight,

>= pivot 3. Start right to left, find 1st entry that is <= pivot 4. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 5. Continue left and right searches The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 6. Find next entry that is >= pivot 7. Find next entry that is <= pivot 8. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 9. Continue left and right searches The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-10 A partitioning of an array during a quick sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved 10. Find next entry that is >= pivot 11. Find next entry that is <= pivot 12. if indexFromLeft is less than indexFromRight, swap the two entries, else search ends 13. place the pivot between the subarrays S1 and S2 by swapping a[indexFromLeft] and a[last]; partition complete 14. Recursively call The Quick Sort © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved •Pivot selection goal • Subarrays each have the same or nearly the same number of entries •Pivot selection strategy • Last entry • First entry • Middle entry • Median-of-three The Quick Sort FIGURE 11-11 Median-of-three pivot selection © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Quick Sort • Analysis – Partitioning is an O(n) task – There are either log2 n or 1 + log2 n levels of recursive calls to quickSort. • We conclude – Worst case O(n2) – Average case O(n log n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Divide and conquer algorithm that is efficient on large number of items – No additional memory required • Cons – Worst case O(n2) – Does not preserve relative order of items with equal keys (not stable) The Radix Sort • Different from other sorts – Does not compare entries in an array • Begins by organizing data (say strings) according to least significant letters – Then combine the groups • Next form groups using next least signficant letter © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Radix Sort FIGURE 11-13 A radix sort of eight integers © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved The Radix Sort • Analysis – Requires n moves each time it forms groups – n moves to combine again into one group – Performs these 2 × n moves d times – Thus requires 2 × n × d moves – More appropriate for chain of linked lists than for an array • Radix sort is of order O(n) © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved • Pros – Radix sort is of order O(n) – Often faster than merge sort or quick sort for large number of items – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – Time complexity grows linearly with the number of digits – Requires same size keys, with standard way of breaking the keys into pieces A Comparison of Sorting Algorithms FIGURE 11-14 Approximate growth rates of time required for eight sorting algorithms © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights reserved End Chapter 11 © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ. All rights

quick sort for large number of items – Preserves relative order of items with equal keys (stable) • Cons – Time complexity grows linearly with the number of digits – Requires same size keys, with standard way of breaking the keys into pieces A Comparison of Sorting Algorithms FIGURE 11-14 Approximate growth rates of time required for eight sorting algorithms © 2017 Pearson Education, Hoboken, NJ.
